item.title: SAR-LiteDETR: A Lightweight Detector for Small Ships in SAR Images
item.dc:creator: Zhen Yang
Haowei Chen
Zhijian Yin
Xin Zhou
Lijun Deng
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Small ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is challenged by speckle noise, strong scale variation, and complex sea clutter, especially under practical efficiency requirements for SAR-based maritime surveillance. We propose SAR-LiteDETR, a lightweight transformer-based detector tailored for maritime SAR scenes. SAR-LiteDETR includes three lightweight plug-and-play modules: PConv-Blocks, an Attention-Guided Feature Pyramid Network (AG-FPN), and a Channel-wise Multi-Branch Fusion Block (CMBF). These three modules improve backbone efficiency and small-target sensitivity, stabilize multi-scale feature alignment under clutter, and adaptively fuse cross-level features to suppress sea clutter and alleviate feature dilution, respectively, while keeping computational cost low. Experiments on the SSDD, HRSID, and LS-SSDD datasets show that SAR-LiteDETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art and baseline detectors. In particular, it achieves A P S AP_{S} and precision of 69.6%/97.0% on SSDD and 54.2%/93.5% on HRSID, respectively, while requiring only 42.1 GFLOPs. The results indicate that SAR-LiteDETR provides a favorable balance between detection accuracy and efficiency for near real-time SAR-based maritime surveillance. Published: 2026-05-04T19:36:08+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.817 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhen Yang; Haowei Chen; Zhijian Yin; Xin Zhou; Lijun Deng

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3689950

Relevance: 0.817 (must_read)

Small ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is challenged by speckle noise, strong scale variation, and complex sea clutter, especially under practical efficiency requirements for SAR-based maritime surveillance. We propose SAR-LiteDETR, a lightweight transformer-based detector tailored for maritime SAR scenes. SAR-LiteDETR includes three lightweight plug-and-play modules: PConv-Blocks, an Attention-Guided Feature Pyramid Network (AG-FPN), and a Channel-wise Multi-Branch Fusion Block (CMBF). These three modules improve backbone efficiency and small-target sensitivity, stabilize multi-scale feature alignment under clutter, and adaptively fuse cross-level features to suppress sea clutter and alleviate feature dilution, respectively, while keeping computational cost low. Experiments on the SSDD, HRSID, and LS-SSDD datasets show that SAR-LiteDETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art and baseline detectors. In particular, it achieves A P S AP_{S} and precision of 69.6%/97.0% on SSDD and 54.2%/93.5% on HRSID, respectively, while requiring only 42.1 GFLOPs. The results indicate that SAR-LiteDETR provides a favorable balance between detection accuracy and efficiency for near real-time SAR-based maritime surveillance.

item.title: Scale-aware Prompting with Optimal Transport for Remote Sensing Image Captioning
item.dc:creator: Cheng Zhang
Zhongle Ren
Biao Hou
Jiawei Ning
Kai Wang
Weibin Li
Licheng Jiao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Remote sensing image captioning is a multimodal foundation task for fine-grained understanding of remote sensing images. However, remote sensing images contain complex scenes and rich objects, it is very challenging to accurately describe the objects in the scene with their attributes and dependencies. To address these issues, the article proposes a novel scale-aware prompting with optimal transport (SPOT) to learn effective multiscale features under diverse scenes, and to build fine-grained cross-modal alignment between semantic features and linguistic words during caption generation. Specifically, a scale-aware prompt extractor is constructed to explore feature integrations in complex scenes through learning prompts that query multi-scale features, and to enhance the representation of attributes and dependencies for objects by embedding positional relations. Besides, a fine-grained cross-modal alignment is designed to dynamically match image feature representations and textual semantics through optimal transport. Through the above manner, the model can learn effective language-aligned feature representations for caption generation. Finally, a caption Transformer with causal self-attention is introduced to generate accurate captions for remote sensing scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public datasets, with the superiority of the proposed method further demonstrated by ablating the role of each component. Published: 2026-05-04T19:38:26+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Cheng Zhang; Zhongle Ren; Biao Hou; Jiawei Ning; Kai Wang; Weibin Li; Licheng Jiao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3687442

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Remote sensing image captioning is a multimodal foundation task for fine-grained understanding of remote sensing images. However, remote sensing images contain complex scenes and rich objects, it is very challenging to accurately describe the objects in the scene with their attributes and dependencies. To address these issues, the article proposes a novel scale-aware prompting with optimal transport (SPOT) to learn effective multiscale features under diverse scenes, and to build fine-grained cross-modal alignment between semantic features and linguistic words during caption generation. Specifically, a scale-aware prompt extractor is constructed to explore feature integrations in complex scenes through learning prompts that query multi-scale features, and to enhance the representation of attributes and dependencies for objects by embedding positional relations. Besides, a fine-grained cross-modal alignment is designed to dynamically match image feature representations and textual semantics through optimal transport. Through the above manner, the model can learn effective language-aligned feature representations for caption generation. Finally, a caption Transformer with causal self-attention is introduced to generate accurate captions for remote sensing scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public datasets, with the superiority of the proposed method further demonstrated by ablating the role of each component.

item.title: MSP-MAE: Multi-Scale Perceptive Masked Autoencoder for Self-Supervised SAR Target Recognition
item.dc:creator: Xunyang Wan
Xiaolin Du
Xiaolong Chen
Guolong Cui
Jibin Zheng
Mengjiao Tang
Wenming Ma
Kaige Hou
Jinwei Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: The masked autoencoder (MAE) is a self-supervised learning model that reconstructs masked patches of an input image. Its pre-trained encoder significantly enhances downstream tasks, even surpassing supervised methods. This is particularly important for synthetic aperture radar (SAR) target recognition because labeled SAR images are scarce. Leveraging large amounts of unlabeled SAR data for network pre-training mitigates this challenge. Therefore, by combining the scale-fusion spatial-channel attention (SF-SCA) and multi-head self-attention (MHSA) modules, the multi-scale perceptive convolutional transformer (MSP-CT) is proposed as the backbone of the multi-scale perceptive MAE (MSP-MAE) for SAR target recognition tasks. The MHSA module exhibits locality and sparsity in shallow features, thereby introducing redundancy in global dependency modeling. In contrast, the SF-SCA module facilitates the interaction of localized and sparse patches through a sliding window. Therefore, the MSP-CT architecture employs alternating stacks of SF-SCA and MHSA blocks to balance the ability of the model to extract local and global features. The experimental results show that MSP-MAE pre-training can effectively improve the performance of the downstream task and can greatly alleviate the problem of insufficient labeled samples. Published: 2026-05-04T19:36:08+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.807 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xunyang Wan; Xiaolin Du; Xiaolong Chen; Guolong Cui; Jibin Zheng; Mengjiao Tang; Wenming Ma; Kaige Hou; Jinwei Wang

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3690033

Relevance: 0.807 (must_read)

The masked autoencoder (MAE) is a self-supervised learning model that reconstructs masked patches of an input image. Its pre-trained encoder significantly enhances downstream tasks, even surpassing supervised methods. This is particularly important for synthetic aperture radar (SAR) target recognition because labeled SAR images are scarce. Leveraging large amounts of unlabeled SAR data for network pre-training mitigates this challenge. Therefore, by combining the scale-fusion spatial-channel attention (SF-SCA) and multi-head self-attention (MHSA) modules, the multi-scale perceptive convolutional transformer (MSP-CT) is proposed as the backbone of the multi-scale perceptive MAE (MSP-MAE) for SAR target recognition tasks. The MHSA module exhibits locality and sparsity in shallow features, thereby introducing redundancy in global dependency modeling. In contrast, the SF-SCA module facilitates the interaction of localized and sparse patches through a sliding window. Therefore, the MSP-CT architecture employs alternating stacks of SF-SCA and MHSA blocks to balance the ability of the model to extract local and global features. The experimental results show that MSP-MAE pre-training can effectively improve the performance of the downstream task and can greatly alleviate the problem of insufficient labeled samples.

item.title: Fine-grained ship classification of remote sensing images based on class discrepancy learning network
item.dc:creator: Xuanyu Zhang
Jie Geng
Wen Jiang
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Remote sensing imagery provides critical support for maritime situational awareness, vessel monitoring, and coastal management. Fine-grained classification of ships in such imagery aims to distinguish visually similar vessel types (e.g., cargo ships, oilers, and fishing boats), which is challenging due to intra-class variation caused by different sensors, observation angles, and complex backgrounds. To address this issue, we propose a class-discrepancy learning network (CDLNet) specifically designed for ship categories exhibiting imbalanced intra-class variation. An intra-class variation index is formulated to quantitatively assess category-specific diversity and guides a targeted adaptive learning strategy to enhance category-level discriminability. For classes with substantial appearance variations, a causally intervened masked attention module is introduced to adaptively capture diverse intra-class representations. The proposed method is particularly effective in remote sensing contexts, where diverse acquisition conditions significantly impact recognition performance. Extensive experiments conducted on two benchmark datasets for fine-grained ship classification in remote sensing imagery demonstrate the superior performance and generalization capability of the proposed method. On the FGSC-23 dataset, our method achieves 93.23% overall accuracy, surpassing the current state-of-the-art methods, including RGCRL-Net and other competitive baselines. The source code is available at https://github.com/MIPCLab/CDLNetcode . Published: 2026-05-05T20:46:13+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xuanyu Zhang; Jie Geng; Wen Jiang

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.051

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Remote sensing imagery provides critical support for maritime situational awareness, vessel monitoring, and coastal management. Fine-grained classification of ships in such imagery aims to distinguish visually similar vessel types (e.g., cargo ships, oilers, and fishing boats), which is challenging due to intra-class variation caused by different sensors, observation angles, and complex backgrounds. To address this issue, we propose a class-discrepancy learning network (CDLNet) specifically designed for ship categories exhibiting imbalanced intra-class variation. An intra-class variation index is formulated to quantitatively assess category-specific diversity and guides a targeted adaptive learning strategy to enhance category-level discriminability. For classes with substantial appearance variations, a causally intervened masked attention module is introduced to adaptively capture diverse intra-class representations. The proposed method is particularly effective in remote sensing contexts, where diverse acquisition conditions significantly impact recognition performance. Extensive experiments conducted on two benchmark datasets for fine-grained ship classification in remote sensing imagery demonstrate the superior performance and generalization capability of the proposed method. On the FGSC-23 dataset, our method achieves 93.23% overall accuracy, surpassing the current state-of-the-art methods, including RGCRL-Net and other competitive baselines. The source code is available at https://github.com/MIPCLab/CDLNetcode .

item.title: FinePruner: Unbiased Attention-Head-Level Fine-grained Token Reduction for Efficient Inference of Large Vision-Language Models
item.dc:creator: Zishuo Wang
Xiangtian Zheng
Yuxin Peng
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) suffer from the high computational cost of the attention mechanism caused by the large number of visual tokens. Token reduction has emerged as a promising approach to reduce the complexity by eliminating redundant visual tokens. However, existing token reduction methods struggle to preserve task-relevant tokens and eliminate irrelevant ones. This is due to the attention biases of LVLMs, where tokens with high attention scores are not always the critical ones. Such biases force existing methods into a dilemma: they face either high performance degradation or limited inference acceleration. This issue becomes more severe in fine-grained perception tasks, which rely heavily on the fine-grained information stored in specific visual tokens. To address the above issue, we propose an unbiased fine-grained token reduction method named FinePruner, which explores the attention patterns of LVLMs at the attention-head-level to mitigate the interference of attention biases. Concretely, we first conducted comparative studies to validate the impact of tokens corresponding to visual objects on final task performance, which established the conclusion that these tokens should be preserved while others can be pruned. Also, a series of visualizations unveils the changing patterns of LVLMs’ attention biases across layers and attention heads. Based on the patterns of attention biases, the pipeline of FinePruner is divided into two stages. The first stage, named Instruction-Agnostic Clustering, clusters visual tokens into groups according to their embeddings to exclude the attention biases. The second stage, named Attention-Refined Pruning, selects attention heads with less bias by the divergence, which are used to identify the preserved tokens. Experiments on VQA benchmarks and fine-grained benchmarks demonstrate that our FinePruner achieves better accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs than state-of-the-art methods. The code is available at https: //github.co... Published: 2026-05-05T20:05:03+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zishuo Wang; Xiangtian Zheng; Yuxin Peng

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3687073

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) suffer from the high computational cost of the attention mechanism caused by the large number of visual tokens. Token reduction has emerged as a promising approach to reduce the complexity by eliminating redundant visual tokens. However, existing token reduction methods struggle to preserve task-relevant tokens and eliminate irrelevant ones. This is due to the attention biases of LVLMs, where tokens with high attention scores are not always the critical ones. Such biases force existing methods into a dilemma: they face either high performance degradation or limited inference acceleration. This issue becomes more severe in fine-grained perception tasks, which rely heavily on the fine-grained information stored in specific visual tokens. To address the above issue, we propose an unbiased fine-grained token reduction method named FinePruner, which explores the attention patterns of LVLMs at the attention-head-level to mitigate the interference of attention biases. Concretely, we first conducted comparative studies to validate the impact of tokens corresponding to visual objects on final task performance, which established the conclusion that these tokens should be preserved while others can be pruned. Also, a series of visualizations unveils the changing patterns of LVLMs’ attention biases across layers and attention heads. Based on the patterns of attention biases, the pipeline of FinePruner is divided into two stages. The first stage, named Instruction-Agnostic Clustering, clusters visual tokens into groups according to their embeddings to exclude the attention biases. The second stage, named Attention-Refined Pruning, selects attention heads with less bias by the divergence, which are used to identify the preserved tokens. Experiments on VQA benchmarks and fine-grained benchmarks demonstrate that our FinePruner achieves better accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs than state-of-the-art methods. The code is available at https: //github.co...

item.title: Integrating Pseudo-Supervision and Spatial Constraints for Efficient Clustering of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data
item.dc:creator: Zhe Cao
Tao Zhang
Zihua Zhao
Haonan Xin
Rong Wang
Feiping Nie
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Multimodal remote sensing (RS) clustering synergistically integrates multi-dimensional information, effectively addressing the representational limitations of single-modality data. This integration offers essential technical support for fine-grained recognition and accurate interpretation of ground objects in complex scenarios. However, existing methods still face several challenges, including insufficient utilization of spatial information, limited ability to extract consistent information due to inter-modal heterogeneity, and low efficiency when handling large-scale and complex datasets. To address these issues, we propose an Integrating Pseudo-Supervision and Spatial Constraints for Efficient Clustering (PSSC) of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data model. The proposed method begins by constructing spatial bipartite graphs from multimodal data to fully exploit spatial information while reducing computational complexity. These graphs are then stacked into a third-order tensor, upon which a robust denoised representation is learned to suppress noise and preserve the core structural characteristics of the multimodal inputs. Based on this clean tensor, PSSC captures cross-modal consistency by minimizing the tensor nuclear norm within the low-rank space. To further enhance clustering efficiency and accuracy, a region homogeneity-constrained rapid label generation strategy is proposed, which leverages high-confidence pseudo-supervision information from homogeneous regions to iteratively refine clustering labels, thereby significantly reducing computational overhead. Extensive experiments on real-world multimodal datasets validate the effectiveness and superior performance of the proposed method. Published: 2026-05-05T20:04:45+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhe Cao; Tao Zhang; Zihua Zhao; Haonan Xin; Rong Wang; Feiping Nie

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3690673

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Multimodal remote sensing (RS) clustering synergistically integrates multi-dimensional information, effectively addressing the representational limitations of single-modality data. This integration offers essential technical support for fine-grained recognition and accurate interpretation of ground objects in complex scenarios. However, existing methods still face several challenges, including insufficient utilization of spatial information, limited ability to extract consistent information due to inter-modal heterogeneity, and low efficiency when handling large-scale and complex datasets. To address these issues, we propose an Integrating Pseudo-Supervision and Spatial Constraints for Efficient Clustering (PSSC) of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data model. The proposed method begins by constructing spatial bipartite graphs from multimodal data to fully exploit spatial information while reducing computational complexity. These graphs are then stacked into a third-order tensor, upon which a robust denoised representation is learned to suppress noise and preserve the core structural characteristics of the multimodal inputs. Based on this clean tensor, PSSC captures cross-modal consistency by minimizing the tensor nuclear norm within the low-rank space. To further enhance clustering efficiency and accuracy, a region homogeneity-constrained rapid label generation strategy is proposed, which leverages high-confidence pseudo-supervision information from homogeneous regions to iteratively refine clustering labels, thereby significantly reducing computational overhead. Extensive experiments on real-world multimodal datasets validate the effectiveness and superior performance of the proposed method.

item.title: Continual Test-Time Adaptive Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images via Spectral Routing and Historical Reconstruction
item.dc:creator: Mingjing Yong
Nanqing Liu
Sen Lei
Jie Pan
Xue Yang
Heng-Chao Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Remote sensing object detection (RSOD) remains challenging due to significant domain shifts caused by variations in sensors, geographic regions, illumination, weather, and object scales, which severely degrade model performance when deployed on unseen target domains. While test-time adaptation (TTA) enables model updating using unlabeled test data, existing methods typically assume relatively stable target distributions and struggle under the dynamic and sequential conditions of remote sensing scenarios. Continual test-time adaptation for object detection (CTAOD) further extends TTA to streaming test data, but current approaches often rely on global updates with fixed criteria, resulting in noisy feature propagation, error accumulation, and catastrophic forgetting. In this paper, we propose CTAOD-RS, a novel framework for continual test-time adaptive object detection in remote sensing imagery. The proposed framework leverages Spectral Routing Adaptation to decompose features into frequency and spatial components, suppressing noise and clutter, and employs History-Informed Reconstruction to dynamically determine when model adaptation is necessary, avoiding unnecessary updates and mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments on benchmark RSOD datasets, including the newly introduced DIOR-C corrupted benchmark designed to simulate realistic remote sensing domain shifts, demonstrate superior detection accuracy and robustness compared to existing TTA and CTAOD methods, while maintaining high efficiency. The code will be released at https://github.com/rosou1/CTAOD-RS. Published: 2026-05-04T19:38:04+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mingjing Yong; Nanqing Liu; Sen Lei; Jie Pan; Xue Yang; Heng-Chao Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3689532

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

Remote sensing object detection (RSOD) remains challenging due to significant domain shifts caused by variations in sensors, geographic regions, illumination, weather, and object scales, which severely degrade model performance when deployed on unseen target domains. While test-time adaptation (TTA) enables model updating using unlabeled test data, existing methods typically assume relatively stable target distributions and struggle under the dynamic and sequential conditions of remote sensing scenarios. Continual test-time adaptation for object detection (CTAOD) further extends TTA to streaming test data, but current approaches often rely on global updates with fixed criteria, resulting in noisy feature propagation, error accumulation, and catastrophic forgetting. In this paper, we propose CTAOD-RS, a novel framework for continual test-time adaptive object detection in remote sensing imagery. The proposed framework leverages Spectral Routing Adaptation to decompose features into frequency and spatial components, suppressing noise and clutter, and employs History-Informed Reconstruction to dynamically determine when model adaptation is necessary, avoiding unnecessary updates and mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments on benchmark RSOD datasets, including the newly introduced DIOR-C corrupted benchmark designed to simulate realistic remote sensing domain shifts, demonstrate superior detection accuracy and robustness compared to existing TTA and CTAOD methods, while maintaining high efficiency. The code will be released at https://github.com/rosou1/CTAOD-RS.

item.title: Division-to-Fusion: Foreground-Background Semantics Decoupling and Coupling for Image-Text Retrieval
item.dc:creator: Xiaolin Wang
Zheng Liu
Junhao Xu
Shuxiang Meng
Zhini Cai
item.prism:publicationName: Information Fusion
item.description: As salient foreground semantics and contextual background semantics in images and texts may inherently interweave with each other, their boundaries remain unclear, which poses a significant challenge to cross-modal similarity learning and becomes a performance bottleneck in image-text retrieval (ITR). To make ITR model conform to human multimodal perception, there are two key problems: (1) Dividing foreground and background semantics to obtain clear boundaries, and (2) Fusing them to compute accurate cross-modal similarity. To address these problems, we propose Foreground-Background Semantics Decoupling and Coupling (FBDC) network, which is a new research paradigm for ITR called Division-to-Fusion. FBDC comprises two modules: Foreground-background decoupling (Module 1) and Foreground-background coupling (Module 2). In Module 1, foreground and background semantic elements are divided under the guidance of a negative saliency strategy. For each semantic element, a larger similarity drop after masking indicates higher foreground membership, whereas lower similarity with the global feature indicates higher background membership. Building upon Module 1, in module 2, foreground and background semantic elements are fused with a step-wise coupling strategy to obtain image-text similarity. First, cross-modal contexts are integrated with their corresponding foreground or background semantic elements to achieve foreground or background level alignment. Second, enhanced by cross-modal contexts, the foreground and background semantic elements are integrated to achieve image-text alignment. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed FBDC network over state-of-the-art ITR methods. Published: 2026-05-04T21:31:25+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaolin Wang; Zheng Liu; Junhao Xu; Shuxiang Meng; Zhini Cai

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104447

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

As salient foreground semantics and contextual background semantics in images and texts may inherently interweave with each other, their boundaries remain unclear, which poses a significant challenge to cross-modal similarity learning and becomes a performance bottleneck in image-text retrieval (ITR). To make ITR model conform to human multimodal perception, there are two key problems: (1) Dividing foreground and background semantics to obtain clear boundaries, and (2) Fusing them to compute accurate cross-modal similarity. To address these problems, we propose Foreground-Background Semantics Decoupling and Coupling (FBDC) network, which is a new research paradigm for ITR called Division-to-Fusion. FBDC comprises two modules: Foreground-background decoupling (Module 1) and Foreground-background coupling (Module 2). In Module 1, foreground and background semantic elements are divided under the guidance of a negative saliency strategy. For each semantic element, a larger similarity drop after masking indicates higher foreground membership, whereas lower similarity with the global feature indicates higher background membership. Building upon Module 1, in module 2, foreground and background semantic elements are fused with a step-wise coupling strategy to obtain image-text similarity. First, cross-modal contexts are integrated with their corresponding foreground or background semantic elements to achieve foreground or background level alignment. Second, enhanced by cross-modal contexts, the foreground and background semantic elements are integrated to achieve image-text alignment. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed FBDC network over state-of-the-art ITR methods.

item.title: Learning to Correct Cross-Modality Bounding Box Shifts in Drone-Based RGBT Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhang
Wen Yang
Chang Xu
Qian Hu
Fang Xu
Gui-Song Xia
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Drone-based RGBT object detection plays a crucial role in many around-the-clock applications such as search & rescue. However, real-world drone-viewed RGBT data suffers from the prominent position shift problem, i.e., the position of a tiny object differs greatly in different modalities. Existing methods simply rely on implicit learning to predict offsets and perform alignment. However, it is challenging to obtain reliable offset predictions due to the lack of supervision; moreover, detectors tend to be biased towards the aligned modality since the other modality is learned with confusing ground-truth bounding boxes, making the offset prediction non-trivial. In this paper, we propose to address the prominent position shift with cross-modality bounding box correction, which exploits cross-modal predictions to correct shifted ground-truth bounding boxes, providing explicit supervision and thereby enhancing the multimodal representation learning. Particularly, we leverage cross-modal knowledge by framing cross-modality bounding box correction into an unsupervised domain adaptation setting and design a progressive bounding box correction strategy, effectively improving correction results. With informative representations of both modalities established, we perform shifted window-based cascaded alignment to mine long-range dependencies between spatially unaligned features and effectively align multimodal features. Our method exceeds the state-of-the-art methods by 4.87 and 5.30 mAP50, respectively, on the RGBTDronePerson dataset and the shifted subset of DroneVehicle. Moreover, we devise a metric named aSim to evaluate the correction performance, and we boost the precision of the sensed modality ground truth by 30.04 points on RGBTDronePerson and 10.29 points on the shifted subset of DroneVehicle. Published: 2026-05-04T19:38:04+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yan Zhang; Wen Yang; Chang Xu; Qian Hu; Fang Xu; Gui-Song Xia

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3690161

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Drone-based RGBT object detection plays a crucial role in many around-the-clock applications such as search & rescue. However, real-world drone-viewed RGBT data suffers from the prominent position shift problem, i.e., the position of a tiny object differs greatly in different modalities. Existing methods simply rely on implicit learning to predict offsets and perform alignment. However, it is challenging to obtain reliable offset predictions due to the lack of supervision; moreover, detectors tend to be biased towards the aligned modality since the other modality is learned with confusing ground-truth bounding boxes, making the offset prediction non-trivial. In this paper, we propose to address the prominent position shift with cross-modality bounding box correction, which exploits cross-modal predictions to correct shifted ground-truth bounding boxes, providing explicit supervision and thereby enhancing the multimodal representation learning. Particularly, we leverage cross-modal knowledge by framing cross-modality bounding box correction into an unsupervised domain adaptation setting and design a progressive bounding box correction strategy, effectively improving correction results. With informative representations of both modalities established, we perform shifted window-based cascaded alignment to mine long-range dependencies between spatially unaligned features and effectively align multimodal features. Our method exceeds the state-of-the-art methods by 4.87 and 5.30 mAP50, respectively, on the RGBTDronePerson dataset and the shifted subset of DroneVehicle. Moreover, we devise a metric named aSim to evaluate the correction performance, and we boost the precision of the sensed modality ground truth by 30.04 points on RGBTDronePerson and 10.29 points on the shifted subset of DroneVehicle.

item.title: HyperTea: Hypergraph-based temporal enhancement and alignment network for moving infrared small target detection
item.dc:creator: Zhaoyuan Qi
Weihua Gao
Wenlong Niu
Jie Tang
Yun Li
Xiaodong Peng
item.prism:publicationName: Knowledge-Based Systems
item.description: In practical application, moving infrared small target detection (MIRSTD) remains highly challenging owing to the target’s small size, low intensity, and complex motion pattern. Existing methods typically model low-order correlations between features and perform feature extraction and enhancement within a single temporal scale. Although hypergraphs have been widely used for high-order correlation learning, they have received limited attention in MIRSTD. To leverage the potential of hypergraphs and enhance multi-timescale feature representation, we propose HyperTea, a framework that integrates global and local temporal perspectives to effectively model high-order spatiotemporal correlations of features. HyperTea consists of three modules: the global temporal enhancement module (GTEM), which realizes global temporal context enhancement through semantic aggregation and propagation; local temporal enhancement module (LTEM) is designed to capture local motion patterns between adjacent frames and then enhance local temporal context; temporal alignment module (TAM) to address potential cross-scale feature misalignment. To the best of our knowledge, HyperTea is the first to integrate convolutional, recurrent, and hypergraph neural networks for MIRSTD, significantly improving detection performance. Experiments on DAUB and IRDST demonstrate its state-of-the-art performance. Code and weights will be available after the paper has been accepted. Published: 2026-05-04T21:28:48+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.787 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhaoyuan Qi; Weihua Gao; Wenlong Niu; Jie Tang; Yun Li; Xiaodong Peng

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116099

Relevance: 0.787 (must_read)

In practical application, moving infrared small target detection (MIRSTD) remains highly challenging owing to the target’s small size, low intensity, and complex motion pattern. Existing methods typically model low-order correlations between features and perform feature extraction and enhancement within a single temporal scale. Although hypergraphs have been widely used for high-order correlation learning, they have received limited attention in MIRSTD. To leverage the potential of hypergraphs and enhance multi-timescale feature representation, we propose HyperTea, a framework that integrates global and local temporal perspectives to effectively model high-order spatiotemporal correlations of features. HyperTea consists of three modules: the global temporal enhancement module (GTEM), which realizes global temporal context enhancement through semantic aggregation and propagation; local temporal enhancement module (LTEM) is designed to capture local motion patterns between adjacent frames and then enhance local temporal context; temporal alignment module (TAM) to address potential cross-scale feature misalignment. To the best of our knowledge, HyperTea is the first to integrate convolutional, recurrent, and hypergraph neural networks for MIRSTD, significantly improving detection performance. Experiments on DAUB and IRDST demonstrate its state-of-the-art performance. Code and weights will be available after the paper has been accepted.

item.title: Adaptive Attention Distillation for Robust Few-Shot Segmentation under Environmental Perturbations
item.dc:creator: Qianyu Guo
Jingrong Wu
Jieji Ren
Weifeng Ge
Wenqiang Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Few-shot segmentation (FSS) aims to rapidly learn novel class concepts from limited examples to segment specific targets in unseen images, and has been widely applied in areas such as medical diagnosis and industrial inspection. However, existing studies largely overlook the complex environmental factors encountered in real-world scenarios—such as illumination, background, and camera viewpoint—which can substantially increase the difficulty of test images. As a result, models trained under laboratory conditions often fall short of practical deployment requirements. To bridge this gap, in this paper, an environment-robust FSS setting is introduced that explicitly incorporates challenging test cases arising from complex environments— such as motion blur, small objects, and camouflaged targets—to enhance model’s robustness under realistic, dynamic conditions. An environment-robust FSS benchmark (ER-FSS) is established, covering eight datasets across multiple real-world scenarios. In addition, an Adaptive Attention Distillation (AAD) method is proposed, which repeatedly contrasts and distills key shared semantics between known (support) and unknown (query) images to derive class-specific attention for novel categories. This strengthens the model’s ability to focus on the correct targets in complex environments, thereby improving environmental robustness. Comparative experiments show that AAD improves mIoU by 3.3%–8.5% across all datasets and settings, demonstrating superior performance and strong generalization. The source code and dataset are available at: https://github.com/guoqianyualberta/ Adaptive-Attention-Distillation-for-FSS. Published: 2026-05-05T20:04:45+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.783 (must_read)

Few-shot segmentation (FSS) aims to rapidly learn novel class concepts from limited examples to segment specific targets in unseen images, and has been widely applied in areas such as medical diagnosis and industrial inspection. However, existing studies largely overlook the complex environmental factors encountered in real-world scenarios—such as illumination, background, and camera viewpoint—which can substantially increase the difficulty of test images. An environment-robust FSS benchmark (ER-FSS) is established, covering eight datasets across multiple real-world scenarios. In addition, an Adaptive Attention Distillation (AAD) method is proposed, which repeatedly contrasts and distills key shared semantics between known (support) and unknown (query) images to derive class-specific attention for novel categories. This strengthens the model’s ability to focus on the correct targets in complex environments, thereby improving environmental robustness. Comparative experiments show that AAD improves mIoU by 3.3%–8.5% across all datasets and settings, demonstrating superior performance and strong generalization. The source code and dataset are available at: https://github.com/guoqianyualberta/ Adaptive-Attention-Distillation-for-FSS.

item.title: Sentinel2Cap: A Human-Annotated Benchmark Dataset for Multimodal Remote Sensing Image Captioning
item.dc:creator: Lucrezia Tosato
Gianluca Lombardi
Ronny Hansch
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Image captioning has become an important task in computer vision, enabling models to generate natural language descriptions of visual content. While several datasets exist for natural images and high-resolution optical remote sensing imagery, the availability of captioning datasets for multimodal satellite data remains limited, particularly for SAR imagery and medium-resolution sensors. We introduce Sentinel2Cap, a human-annotated multimodal captioning dataset containing Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 multi-spectral image patches at 10 m and 20 m spatial resolution with diverse land cover compositions. Captions are created manually and carefully validated to ensure both semantic accuracy and linguistic quality. To evaluate Sentinel2Cap, we perform a zero-shot captioning using the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct model across three image modalities: RGB, multi-spectral, and SAR pseudo-RGB representations. Results show that RGB images achieve the highest captioning performance, while SAR images remain more challenging for vision-language models. Providing modality-specific contextual prompts consistently improves performance across all metrics. These findings highlight both the challenges of multimodal remote sensing image captioning and the potential value of human-annotated datasets for advancing research in cross-modal scene understanding. All the material is publicly avaiable. Published: 2026-05-04T22:16:11+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lucrezia Tosato; Gianluca Lombardi; Ronny Hansch

Journal: arXiv

Image captioning has become an important task in computer vision, enabling models to generate natural language descriptions of visual content. While several datasets exist for natural images and high-resolution optical remote sensing imagery, the availability of captioning datasets for multimodal satellite data remains limited, particularly for SAR imagery and medium-resolution sensors. We introduce Sentinel2Cap, a human-annotated multimodal captioning dataset containing Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 multi-spectral image patches at 10 m and 20 m spatial resolution with diverse land cover compositions. Captions are created manually and carefully validated to ensure both semantic accuracy and linguistic quality. To evaluate Sentinel2Cap, we perform a zero-shot captioning using the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct model across three image modalities: RGB, multi-spectral, and SAR pseudo-RGB representations. Results show that RGB images achieve the highest captioning performance, while SAR images remain more challenging for vision-language models. Providing modality-specific contextual prompts consistently improves performance across all metrics. These findings highlight both the challenges of multimodal remote sensing image captioning and the potential value of human-annotated datasets for advancing research in cross-modal scene understanding. All the material is publicly avaiable.

item.title: BSOEDet: Background Suppression and Object Enhancement Detector for UAV Aerial Imagery
item.dc:creator: Shiliang Zhu
Bin Luo
Jun Liu
Zhensong Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), leveraging their flexible low-altitude imaging and real-time data acquisition capabilities, are widely employed for aerial imagery tasks. However, UAV aerial images contain numerous small objects characterized by low pixel proportions and weak features, making them interference to background noise. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a UAV Aerial Imagery Background Suppression and Object Enhancement Detector (BSOEDet). Specifically, an Object and Background Aware Feature Extractor (OBAE) was developed, in which an auxiliary region classifier differentiates between background and object regions, and self-attention and channel attention mechanisms are combined to suppress irrelevant background and enhance object features, thereby maximizing the representation of small object feature. Furthermore, we propose a Deep Semantic Mining Small Object Module (DSMSOM). DSMSOM employs cross-attention to establish cross-spatial semantic links between deep and shallow features. By leveraging scale-invariant features between large and small objects, and semantic correlations among different objects, it mines potential small object features in the shallow layers using the semantic information of deep large objects, and further reinforces these representations with Direction Adaptive Convolution (DAConv). Moreover, a Global–Local Object Distribution Aware Classification Loss ( L G L O D {\mathcal {L}}_{\mathrm {GLOD}} ) is incorporated, in which the average object area and number of the entire train dataset and each batch are statistically computed to dynamically generate classification loss weights, thus enhancing the optimization contribution of small objects and few-shot classes during training. BSOEDet is validated on the VisDrone and UAVDT datasets. n the overall performance metric m A P 50 \mathrm {mAP}_{50} , BSOEDet achieves improvements of 6.5% and 9.4% over the baseline models, and on the small object performance metric m A P s \mathrm {mAP}_{s} , i... Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), leveraging their flexible low-altitude imaging and real-time data acquisition capabilities, are widely employed for aerial imagery tasks. However, UAV aerial images contain numerous small objects characterized by low pixel proportions and weak features, making them interference to background noise. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a UAV Aerial Imagery Background Suppression and Object Enhancement Detector (BSOEDet). Specifically, an Object and Background Aware Feature Extractor (OBAE) was developed, in which an auxiliary region classifier differentiates between background and object regions, and self-attention and channel attention mechanisms are combined to suppress irrelevant background and enhance object features, thereby maximizing the representation of small object feature. Furthermore, we propose a Deep Semantic Mining Small Object Module (DSMSOM). DSMSOM employs cross-attention to establish cross-spatial semantic links between deep and shallow features. By leveraging scale-invariant features between large and small objects, and semantic correlations among different objects, it mines potential small object features in the shallow layers using the semantic information of deep large objects, and further reinforces these representations with Direction Adaptive Convolution (DAConv). Moreover, a Global–Local Object Distribution Aware Classification Loss ( L G L O D {\mathcal {L}}_{\mathrm {GLOD}} ) is incorporated, in which the average object area and number of the entire train dataset and each batch are statistically computed to dynamically generate classification loss weights, thus enhancing the optimization contribution of small objects and few-shot classes during training. BSOEDet is validated on the VisDrone and UAVDT datasets. n the overall performance metric m A P 50 \mathrm {mAP}_{50} , BSOEDet achieves improvements of 6.5% and 9.4% over the baseline models, and on the small object performance metric m A P s \mathrm {mAP}_{s} , i...

item.title: Rethinking Electro-Optical Vision Foundation Models for Remote Sensing Retrieval: A Controlled Comparison with Generalist VFM
item.dc:creator: Hyobin Park
Minseok Seo
Dong-Geol Choi
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Vision foundation models have attracted significant attention for their ability to leverage large-scale unlabeled visual data. This advantage is particularly important in remote sensing, where data acquisition is costly and annotation often requires expert knowledge. Recent electro-optical vision foundation models aim to learn domain-specific representations from remote sensing imagery, but it remains unclear whether they are more effective than strong generalist vision foundation models under retrieval-based evaluation. In this study, we conduct a controlled comparison between representative EO-specific and generalist vision foundation models for remote sensing image retrieval. Using the same datasets, retrieval protocol, and evaluation metric, we evaluate both in-domain performance and cross-scene generalization. Our results show that strong generalist vision foundation models are competitive with, and in some cases outperform, existing EO-specific models. Moreover, EO-specific models often suffer from substantial degradation under cross-scene evaluation, while generalist models show more stable transfer. These findings suggest that EO pretraining alone does not guarantee stronger retrieval-oriented remote sensing representations. We discuss the limitations of current EO-specific pretraining strategies and highlight the need for future EO vision foundation models to better exploit the physical, spatial, spectral, and geographic characteristics of remote sensing imagery. Published: 2026-05-04T07:18:33+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.781 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hyobin Park; Minseok Seo; Dong-Geol Choi

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

Vision foundation models have attracted significant attention for their ability to leverage large-scale unlabeled visual data. This advantage is particularly important in remote sensing, where data acquisition is costly and annotation often requires expert knowledge. Recent electro-optical vision foundation models aim to learn domain-specific representations from remote sensing imagery, but it remains unclear whether they are more effective than strong generalist vision foundation models under retrieval-based evaluation. In this study, we conduct a controlled comparison between representative EO-specific and generalist vision foundation models for remote sensing image retrieval. Using the same datasets, retrieval protocol, and evaluation metric, we evaluate both in-domain performance and cross-scene generalization. Our results show that strong generalist vision foundation models are competitive with, and in some cases outperform, existing EO-specific models. Moreover, EO-specific models often suffer from substantial degradation under cross-scene evaluation, while generalist models show more stable transfer. These findings suggest that EO pretraining alone does not guarantee stronger retrieval-oriented remote sensing representations. We discuss the limitations of current EO-specific pretraining strategies and highlight the need for future EO vision foundation models to better exploit the physical, spatial, spectral, and geographic characteristics of remote sensing imagery.

item.title: Unifying RGB and thermal object detection in one detector
item.dc:creator: Bin Fan
Jinyu Wang
Wei Zhao
Yongjie Chen
Hongmin Liu
item.prism:publicationName: Pattern Recognition
item.description: Object detection in complex real-world environments remains a significant challenge, especially when relying on a single-modality input such as RGB or thermal imagery. This paper proposes Unified Object Detector (UOD), a unified object detection framework that enables accurate RGB, thermal, and RGBT detection using a single set of network parameters. Our key insight reveals that the subtle discriminative features obscured by non-discriminative modality features in either RGB or thermal images can be effectively excavated through cross-modality contrastive learning. Specifically, we design a feature separation framework comprising: 1) Basic Feature Extractor (BFE), 2) Intrinsic Feature Extractor (IFE), and 3) Non-discriminative Modality Feature Extractor (NFE). A cross-modality contrastive learning strategy is proposed between IFE and NFE to decouple modality-invariant intrinsic features from non-discriminative modality features, thus facilitating accurate detection for various kinds of input modalities. Experiments on three benchmarks show that UOD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art single-modality detectors by up to 5% mAP. Furthermore, the architecture-agnostic generalizability of our framework is validated through its successful application to YOLOv5, YOLOv8, and DETR, yielding consistent performance improvements. Code will be released. Published: 2026-05-04T06:21:28+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.779 (must_read)

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Object detection in complex real-world environments remains a significant challenge, especially when relying on a single-modality input such as RGB or thermal imagery. This paper proposes Unified Object Detector (UOD), a unified object detection framework that enables accurate RGB, thermal, and RGBT detection using a single set of network parameters. Our key insight reveals that the subtle discriminative features obscured by non-discriminative modality features in either RGB or thermal images can be effectively excavated through cross-modality contrastive learning. Specifically, we design a feature separation framework comprising: 1) Basic Feature Extractor (BFE), 2) Intrinsic Feature Extractor (IFE), and 3) Non-discriminative Modality Feature Extractor (NFE). A cross-modality contrastive learning strategy is proposed between IFE and NFE to decouple modality-invariant intrinsic features from non-discriminative modality features, thus facilitating accurate detection for various kinds of input modalities. Experiments on three benchmarks show that UOD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art single-modality detectors by up to 5% mAP. Furthermore, the architecture-agnostic generalizability of our framework is validated through its successful application to YOLOv5, YOLOv8, and DETR, yielding consistent performance improvements. Code will be released.

item.title: CD-GAN: Comparative Disentanglement Learning for Zero-Shot Cross-Modal Retrieval
item.dc:creator: Qiang Li
Shihao Wang
Yuhao Liu
Xiaorong Zhu
Shaojin Bai
Weizhi Nie
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Zero-shot cross-modal retrieval (ZS-CMR) is a critical and challenging task due to the heterogeneous gaps and inconsistent distributions between different modalities, as well as the necessity to establish connections between seen and unseen categories. Mainstream approaches map heterogeneous data into a unified subspace by learning a common representation, enabling the measurement of semantic similarity between different modalities through the calculation of Euclidean distance. However, these methods often overlook the fact that only semantically relevant features essential for discrimination are required for effective retrieval, while semantically irrelevant and non-discriminative features can confound the retrieval model. Moreover, existing methods often provide limited explicit modeling of the transfer from seen categories to unseen ones. In this paper, we hypothesize that the original features comprise two complementary components: modality-invariant features and modality-specific features. The former represents features that are shared and semantically relevant across different modalities, whereas the latter encapsulates features that are modality-related but semantically irrelevant. To address these limitations, we propose Comparative Disentanglement Learning (CD-GAN) for ZS-CMR, which disentangles the original features into modality-invariant and modality-specific features, ensuring that the former captures as much semantically relevant information as possible to aid discrimination. Specifically, the disentangled modality-invariant features are combined with modality-specific features from another modality to generate adversarial samples. The adversarial interaction between the generator and the discriminator enhances the generator’s capability to produce adversarial samples, thereby improving the accuracy of feature disentanglement. In addition, we introduce a prototype-guided Pre-Generation Module for settings where class-level semantic side information is ... Zero-shot cross-modal retrieval (ZS-CMR) is a critical and challenging task due to the heterogeneous gaps and inconsistent distributions between different modalities, as well as the necessity to establish connections between seen and unseen categories. Mainstream approaches map heterogeneous data into a unified subspace by learning a common representation, enabling the measurement of semantic similarity between different modalities through the calculation of Euclidean distance. However, these methods often overlook the fact that only semantically relevant features essential for discrimination are required for effective retrieval, while semantically irrelevant and non-discriminative features can confound the retrieval model. Moreover, existing methods often provide limited explicit modeling of the transfer from seen categories to unseen ones. In this paper, we hypothesize that the original features comprise two complementary components: modality-invariant features and modality-specific features. The former represents features that are shared and semantically relevant across different modalities, whereas the latter encapsulates features that are modality-related but semantically irrelevant. To address these limitations, we propose Comparative Disentanglement Learning (CD-GAN) for ZS-CMR, which disentangles the original features into modality-invariant and modality-specific features, ensuring that the former captures as much semantically relevant information as possible to aid discrimination. Specifically, the disentangled modality-invariant features are combined with modality-specific features from another modality to generate adversarial samples. The adversarial interaction between the generator and the discriminator enhances the generator’s capability to produce adversarial samples, thereby improving the accuracy of feature disentanglement. In addition, we introduce a prototype-guided Pre-Generation Module for settings where class-level semantic side information is ...

item.title: SimPB++: Simultaneously Detecting 2D and 3D Objects from Multiple Cameras
item.dc:creator: Yingqi Tang
Zhaotie Meng
Erkang Cheng
Haibin Ling
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Simultaneous perception of 2D objects in perspective view and 3D objects in Bird's Eye View (BEV) is challenging for multi-camera autonomous driving. Existing two-stage pipelines use 2D results only as a one-time cue for 3D detection. We propose SimPB++, which simultaneously detects 2D objects in perspective and 3D objects in BEV from multiple cameras. It unifies both tasks into an end-to-end model with a hybrid decoder architecture, coupling multi-view 2D and 3D decoders interactively. Two novel modules enable deep interaction: Dynamic Query Allocation adaptively assigns 2D queries to 3D candidates, and Adaptive Query Aggregation refines 3D representations using multi-view 2D features, forming a cyclic 3D-2D-3D refinement. For multi-view 2D detection, we use Query-group Attention for intra-group communication. We also design a Crop-and-Scale strategy for long-range perception and a Propagating Denoising strategy with an auxiliary RoI detector. SimPB++ supports mixed supervision with 2D-only and fully annotated data, reducing reliance on expensive 3D labels. Experiments show state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes for both tasks and strong long-range detection (up to 150m) on Argoverse2. Published: 2026-05-03T15:10:38+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yingqi Tang; Zhaotie Meng; Erkang Cheng; Haibin Ling

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Simultaneous perception of 2D objects in perspective view and 3D objects in Bird's Eye View (BEV) is challenging for multi-camera autonomous driving. Existing two-stage pipelines use 2D results only as a one-time cue for 3D detection. We propose SimPB++, which simultaneously detects 2D objects in perspective and 3D objects in BEV from multiple cameras. It unifies both tasks into an end-to-end model with a hybrid decoder architecture, coupling multi-view 2D and 3D decoders interactively. Two novel modules enable deep interaction: Dynamic Query Allocation adaptively assigns 2D queries to 3D candidates, and Adaptive Query Aggregation refines 3D representations using multi-view 2D features, forming a cyclic 3D-2D-3D refinement. For multi-view 2D detection, we use Query-group Attention for intra-group communication. We also design a Crop-and-Scale strategy for long-range perception and a Propagating Denoising strategy with an auxiliary RoI detector. SimPB++ supports mixed supervision with 2D-only and fully annotated data, reducing reliance on expensive 3D labels. Experiments show state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes for both tasks and strong long-range detection (up to 150m) on Argoverse2.

item.title: AS-SAM2: Adaptive Self-correction for Visual Tracking with SAM2
item.dc:creator: Tao Lv
Shenrun Ding
Yuji Wang
Yue Li
Xiaohuan Lu
Youliang Tian
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Visual tracking faces challenges in effectively leveraging long-term video-level contextual information due to error accumulation caused by appearance changes. Recent SAM2-based trackers address this through improved memory management but share a critical limitation: they lack mechanisms to actively detect and rectify tracking errors, allowing localization errors to gradually contaminate the memory. To address this, we propose AS-SAM2, an adaptive self-correction framework that blocks error propagation at three complementary levels by exploiting the capacity of SAM2 to accept various prompts at any frame. At the tracking level, we propose an Appearance Evaluation-based Self-correction (AES) module to identify frames with tracking errors and adopt prompts to correct them, ensuring accurate features in the memory bank for subsequent frames. At the prompt level, to mitigate background interference from box prompts, we propose a Dual-Prompt Denoising (DPD) module that employs negative prompts to enhance foreground-background distinction, thereby improving tracking robustness. At the memory level, to prevent unreliable frames from degrading the memory bank during occlusion, we propose a State-aware Adaptive Memory Selection (SAS) that dynamically switches between short-term and long-term memory based on visibility state estimation. Experimental evaluations on six benchmark datasets demonstrate that AS-SAM2 achieves state-of-the-art results on LaSOT, LaSOText, GOT-10k, and TNL2K without requiring retraining or fine-tuning, outperforming fully supervised trackers in complex scenarios. This zero-shot capability underscores its potential for deployment in real-world dynamic environments where adaptability and robustness are critical. All the source code will be released at https://github.com/oatlvv/AS-SAM2. Published: 2026-05-05T20:04:45+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.776 (must_read)

Authors: Tao Lv; Shenrun Ding; Yuji Wang; Yue Li; Xiaohuan Lu; Youliang Tian

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3690345

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Visual tracking faces challenges in effectively leveraging long-term video-level contextual information due to error accumulation caused by appearance changes. Recent SAM2-based trackers address this through improved memory management but share a critical limitation: they lack mechanisms to actively detect and rectify tracking errors, allowing localization errors to gradually contaminate the memory. To address this, we propose AS-SAM2, an adaptive self-correction framework that blocks error propagation at three complementary levels by exploiting the capacity of SAM2 to accept various prompts at any frame. At the tracking level, we propose an Appearance Evaluation-based Self-correction (AES) module to identify frames with tracking errors and adopt prompts to correct them, ensuring accurate features in the memory bank for subsequent frames. At the prompt level, to mitigate background interference from box prompts, we propose a Dual-Prompt Denoising (DPD) module that employs negative prompts to enhance foreground-background distinction, thereby improving tracking robustness. At the memory level, to prevent unreliable frames from degrading the memory bank during occlusion, we propose a State-aware Adaptive Memory Selection (SAS) that dynamically switches between short-term and long-term memory based on visibility state estimation. Experimental evaluations on six benchmark datasets demonstrate that AS-SAM2 achieves state-of-the-art results on LaSOT, LaSOText, GOT-10k, and TNL2K without requiring retraining or fine-tuning, outperforming fully supervised trackers in complex scenarios. This zero-shot capability underscores its potential for deployment in real-world dynamic environments where adaptability and robustness are critical. All the source code will be released at https://github.com/oatlvv/AS-SAM2.

item.title: LDFE: Laplacian Decoupled Feature Enhancement block for dual-stream CNN-based RGB-IR object detection
item.dc:creator: Wenhao Dong
Xiaoyan Luo
Linlin Yang
Haodong Zhu
Xiaorong Shi
Guodong Guo
Baochang Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: Pattern Recognition
item.description: The complementary information between RGB and IR images can significantly enhance object detection performance under extreme conditions. Existing methods prefer dual-stream CNN backbones built upon YOLO for feature extraction and focus on the design of feature fusion. In this paper, we introduce the Laplacian Decoupled Feature Enhancement block (LDFE) to fuse features from different stages of the dual-stream CNN backbones. By design, LDFE simultaneously considers the characteristics of modalities and structures for feature fusion by employing global-local decomposition, denoising, fusion, and reconstruction, sequentially. The LDFE first separates features into global and local components based on Laplacian Pyramid, and then performs denoising and fusion based on Global State Space Enhancement module (GS 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 E) and Local Convolutional Correlation Enhancement module (LC 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 E) separately. Specifically, the GS 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 E conducts a two-branch architecture for the main and auxiliary modalities. It dynamically suppresses noise in the main modality through cross-modal attention derived from the auxiliary modality, while employing a State Space Model to capture long-range dependencies within the global feature representations of the main modality. To obtain bidirectional interaction, the two modalities systematically alternate their main/auxiliary roles. Moreover, the LC 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 E suppresses noise in local features and leverages spatial and channel dimension along with triple convolution to extract fine-grained details for fusion. These innovative designs achieve a significant performance improvement, with m " role="presentation"> m m AP surpassing the SOTA methods 6.2% " role="presentation"> 6.2% 6.2% , 3.7% " role="presentation"> 3.7% 3.7% , 4.7% " role="presentation"> 4.7% 4.7% , 2.3% " role="presentation"> 2.3% 2.3% , 4.1% " role="presentation"> 4.1% 4.1% and 2.0% " role="presentation"> 2.0% 2.0% on M 3 " role="presentation"> 3 3 FD, DroneVehicle, LLVIP, FLIR-Aligned, KAIST and VEDAI datasets, respectively. Published: 2026-05-05T14:59:55+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.775 (must_read)

Authors: Wenhao Dong; Xiaoyan Luo; Linlin Yang; Haodong Zhu; Xiaorong Shi; Guodong Guo; Baochang Zhang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113935

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

The complementary information between RGB and IR images can significantly enhance object detection performance under extreme conditions. Existing methods prefer dual-stream CNN backbones built upon YOLO for feature extraction and focus on the design of feature fusion. In this paper, we introduce the Laplacian Decoupled Feature Enhancement block (LDFE) to fuse features from different stages of the dual-stream CNN backbones. By design, LDFE simultaneously considers the characteristics of modalities and structures for feature fusion by employing global-local decomposition, denoising, fusion, and reconstruction, sequentially. The LDFE first separates features into global and local components based on Laplacian Pyramid, and then performs denoising and fusion based on Global State Space Enhancement module (GS 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 E) and Local Convolutional Correlation Enhancement module (LC 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 E) separately. Specifically, the GS 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 E conducts a two-branch architecture for the main and auxiliary modalities. It dynamically suppresses noise in the main modality through cross-modal attention derived from the auxiliary modality, while employing a State Space Model to capture long-range dependencies within the global feature representations of the main modality. To obtain bidirectional interaction, the two modalities systematically alternate their main/auxiliary roles. Moreover, the LC 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 E suppresses noise in local features and leverages spatial and channel dimension along with triple convolution to extract fine-grained details for fusion. These innovative designs achieve a significant performance improvement, with m " role="presentation"> m m AP surpassing the SOTA methods 6.2% " role="presentation"> 6.2% 6.2% , 3.7% " role="presentation"> 3.7% 3.7% , 4.7% " role="presentation"> 4.7% 4.7% , 2.3% " role="presentation"> 2.3% 2.3% , 4.1% " role="presentation"> 4.1% 4.1% and 2.0% " role="presentation"> 2.0% 2.0% on M 3 " role="presentation"> 3 3 FD, DroneVehicle, LLVIP, FLIR-Aligned, KAIST and VEDAI datasets, respectively.

item.title: Towards Comprehensive Information-theoretic Multi-view Learning
item.dc:creator: Long Shi
Yunshan Ye
Wenjie Wang
Tao Lei
Yu Zhao
Gang Kou
Badong Chen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Information theory has inspired numerous advancements in multi-view learning. Most multi-view methods incorporating information-theoretic principles rely an assumption called multi-view redundancy which states that common information between views is necessary and sufficient for down-stream tasks. This assumption emphasizes the importance of common information for prediction, but inherently ignores the potential of unique information in each view that could be predictive to the task. In this paper, we propose a comprehensive information-theoretic multi-view learning framework named CIML, which discards the assumption of multi-view redundancy. Specifically, CIML considers the potential predictive capabilities of both common and unique information based on information theory. First, the common representation learning maximizes G´acs-K¨orner common information to extract shared features and then compresses this information to learn task-relevant representations based on the Information Bottleneck (IB). For unique representation learning, IB is employed to achieve the most compressed unique representation for each view while simultaneously minimizing the mutual information between unique and common representations, as well as among different unique representations. Importantly, we theoretically prove that the learned joint representation is predictively sufficient for the downstream task. Extensive experimental results have demonstrated the superiority of our model over several state-of-art methods. The code is released on CIML. Published: 2026-05-04T19:38:26+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.775 (must_read)
